title: Second Chunce

jokes.text: Andy got back from London two days ago and he still has jet lag. It's really annoying. We're on totally different schedules. Last night he mowed the lawn at 2:00 in the morning.
jokes.setup: Jet lag causing schedule conflicts
jokes.punchline: Mowing lawn at 2 AM as the example
jokes.explanation: Perfectly absurd example of jet lag consequences that fits Andy's character. The specificity of lawn mowing at 2 AM makes it funny.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I have jet lag so bad, I mowed the lawn at 2 AM.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: At 2 in the morning?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: The neighbors loved it.

jokes.text: As soon as I wake up, he goes down. Hard.
jokes.setup: Describing Andy falling asleep
jokes.punchline: Phrasing that could be interpreted sexually
jokes.explanation: Mild innuendo that works for April's deadpan delivery but relies on double entendre rather than clever writing.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: He fell asleep during sex. I just laid there thinking, 'Well, this is a new low.' But then I realized... I was kind of into it. Phrasing.

jokes.text: That's my spaghetti, Chewbacca.
jokes.setup: Andy talking in his sleep
jokes.punchline: Random combination of spaghetti and Star Wars reference
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy sleep-talking that combines his love of food and pop culture in a wonderfully absurd way. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: May the forks be with you... I love you, spaghetti...

jokes.text: Were you about to spray me with the hose inside the house?
jokes.setup: April trying to wake Andy up
jokes.punchline: Casual acceptance of indoor hose spraying
jokes.explanation: Good visual comedy setup with Andy's characteristically casual reaction to absurd situation.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Andy, wake up.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What? No, five more minutes.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: We need to go. Now.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Fine, fine. I'm up.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You're not even awake yet.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yes I am. I'm totally awake.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You're still lying down.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Okay, you know what? Spray me with the hose.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Just spray me with the hose. That'll wake me up.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: We're inside.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I know. Do it anyway.

jokes.text: I kinda want you to do it now.
jokes.setup: April about to spray him with hose
jokes.punchline: Andy actually wanting to be sprayed
jokes.explanation: Classic Andy move - turning potential conflict into something he actually wants. Good character moment.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'm going to spray you with this hose.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yes! Do it! I love it!

jokes.text: When she walked past me this morning, I gave her a kind nod.
jokes.setup: Being told to be extra supportive of Leslie
jokes.punchline: Ron's minimal effort being presented as 'already done'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron Swanson moment - his version of being 'extra supportive' is hilariously minimal but presented with complete sincerity.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm already being supportive. I showed up today.

jokes.text: Wine is crying juice.
jokes.setup: Discussing what gift to avoid for Leslie
jokes.punchline: Tom's colorful descriptor for wine
jokes.explanation: Brilliant, concise Tom-ism that perfectly captures both his linguistic style and the situation. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Never get Leslie wine. She'll drink the whole bottle and start talking about how she's gonna run for president again.

jokes.text: A nice candle. I'm screwed.
jokes.setup: Being asked what Leslie wants more than anything
jokes.punchline: Ben's generic answer followed by self-aware panic
jokes.explanation: Relatable husband moment with good self-awareness. The contrast between question and answer works well.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie wants to make the world a better place. She wants to help people and build meaningful relationships.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Oh God, that's what everyone wants. I just described every human being. Why would anyone ask that? What am I doing?

jokes.text: Well, technically, my home away from home is in Zurich. Frank Gehry designed it. But this is nice too.
jokes.setup: Ben calling City Council chambers Jeremy's 'home away from home'
jokes.punchline: Jeremy's casual mention of architect-designed Zurich property
jokes.explanation: Good character establishment for Jeremy's wealth and casual attitude about luxury. The 'but this is nice too' understatement works.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: City Council chambers is your home away from home.

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy
dialogue.line: Well, actually, my home away from home is my architect-designed property in Zurich.

jokes.text: Denial, anger, Internet commenting, cat adoption, African dance, cat returning to the adoption place, watching all the episodes of Murphy Brown, and not giving a flying fart.
jokes.setup: Leslie listing the 'five stages of grief'
jokes.punchline: Increasingly absurd and specific stages, way more than five
jokes.explanation: Excellent Leslie moment that escalates beautifully with specific, character-appropriate absurdities. The cat adoption/return cycle and Murphy Brown are perfect touches.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: The five stages of grief are denial, anger, bargaining, depression, and acceptance.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: But I've identified seven additional stages: negotiating with a higher power you don't believe in, calling your mother, stress-eating an entire rotisserie chicken, Googling 'am I dying,' watching videos of people adopting dogs, considering a complete life overhaul in Costa Rica, and finally, buying a decorative throw pillow that says 'Live, Laugh, Love.'

jokes.text: How many stages is that? I don't know. The point is I'm fine now.
jokes.setup: The overly long list of grief stages
jokes.punchline: Leslie's self-aware but dismissive response
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up that acknowledges the absurdity while maintaining Leslie's character trait of pushing through problems.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: There are actually seven stages of grief, not five. Denial, anger, bargaining, depression, acceptance... and two others I don't remember because I skipped them.

jokes.text: It's like what sir Ian McKellen said to me the day I sold my boat to Karl Lagerfeld: 'Parting is such sweet sorrow.'
jokes.setup: Jeremy trying to be supportive about Leslie leaving
jokes.punchline: Casually dropping celebrity name-drops in impossible scenario
jokes.explanation: Good Jeremy character moment showcasing his absurd wealth and connections. The casual delivery sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy
dialogue.line: I'm really going to miss you, Leslie. But hey, look on the bright side – at least you'll finally get to meet Beyoncé.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Jeremy, I'm moving to Colorado, not to heaven.

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy
dialogue.line: Right, right. Well, when you're there, maybe you'll run into Ryan Gosling at the grocery store or something.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's... that's not how anything works.

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy
dialogue.line: I'm just trying to stay positive. Plus, Oprah's probably in Denver all the time.

jokes.text: Oh, my God. What is your life?
jokes.setup: Jeremy's ridiculous story
jokes.punchline: Leslie's bewildered but direct response
jokes.explanation: Perfect straight-man reaction that voices what audience is thinking. Economy of words makes it punchy.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I have no idea what you just said, but I'm 87% sure it was insane.

jokes.text: I'm sorry. Was I singing Goodbye Yellow Brick Road out loud?
jokes.setup: Leslie being caught in a moment of sadness
jokes.punchline: Self-aware acknowledgment of obvious emotional state
jokes.explanation: Mild moment that shows Leslie's self-awareness about her obvious emotional state. Song choice is appropriate for leaving.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm fine. Everything is fine. I'm just going to go to my office and be fine alone.

jokes.text: I studied opera at the Sorbonne. I'd be happy to harmonize.
jokes.setup: Leslie mentioning singing
jokes.punchline: Jeremy's immediate offer with casual name-drop of prestigious education
jokes.explanation: Consistent Jeremy character work showing his privileged background and eagerness to show off.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I love singing.

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy
dialogue.line: I went to Northwestern. I can teach you.

jokes.text: Dear Tom: I win. You suck. Turn note over. You still suck. Turn note over.
jokes.setup: Professional business note from Dr. Saperstein
jokes.punchline: Childish insults with repetitive structure
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with the repeated 'turn note over' structure. Captures Saperstein's petty vindictiveness well.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Saperstein
dialogue.line: You're a poopy-head. You're a doo-doo face. You're a buttmunch.

jokes.text: What if I gave you 5 bucks to put a bag of poop in Saperstein's car?
jokes.setup: Lawyer explaining how successful people use money
jokes.punchline: Tom's immediate childish application of the concept
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom response showing his immaturity and literal interpretation of business advice.

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: Successful people use money to solve problems.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: So I should buy a jet ski?

jokes.text: Counter-offer: Why don't you just do it for the story?
jokes.setup: Lawyer asking for $10,000 for the poop task
jokes.punchline: Tom trying to negotiate down to 'for the story'
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation that shows Tom's delusional understanding of motivation. 'For the story' is a perfect Tom phrase.

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: That'll be $10,000.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Okay, what if I do it for the story?

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: For the exposure?

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: For the clout?

jokes.text: That is the most symmetrical fetus I've ever seen. This could be a superhero.
jokes.setup: Looking at ultrasound images
jokes.punchline: Professional doctor making superhero assessment based on symmetry
jokes.explanation: Funny unexpected medical assessment that feels both professional and completely absurd. The symmetry detail sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Saperstein
dialogue.line: Look at the symmetry on this one. This kid's gonna be a superhero.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What? Based on what?

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Saperstein
dialogue.line: Symmetry. It's the most important thing. Asymmetrical people become villains. It's science.

jokes.text: I'm loveable!
jokes.setup: Ben and Chris expressing love for him despite his destroying Tom's business
jokes.punchline: Saperstein's delighted self-assessment
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Saperstein's need for approval despite his antagonistic behavior.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Saperstein
dialogue.line: I'm like the most beloved man in this town. Everybody loves me.

jokes.text: Is there an option other than yes or no?
jokes.setup: Whether to know the baby's sex
jokes.punchline: Ben's desperate search for a third option in a binary choice
jokes.explanation: Shows Ben's overthinking nature well. The desperation in trying to find a middle ground is relatable and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: What if we don't find out? We could be surprised.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ben, there are two options.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I know, but what if there's a third thing?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: There's not a third thing.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: But what if science has come up with something new? A third gender baby?

jokes.text: Wait, no, I have to pee. Wait, no, I have to barf. Actually, all three. Being pregnant is great.
jokes.setup: Ann saying she's hungry
jokes.punchline: Rapid escalation of pregnancy symptoms with sarcastic conclusion
jokes.explanation: Relatable pregnancy humor with good escalation pattern. The sarcastic conclusion ties it together.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I'm hungry.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Actually, I'm starving. And nauseous. And my feet hurt. And I'm exhausted. And everything smells like pennies. And I think I just felt the baby kick, or maybe I'm just having a stroke. But sure, let's go with pregnancy.

jokes.text: No, it's not, you boring grandpa.
jokes.setup: Someone saying 'Good to be Larry'
jokes.punchline: Tom's immediate harsh contradiction and insult
jokes.explanation: Classic Tom dismissiveness toward Jerry/Larry. The 'boring grandpa' insult is perfectly Tom.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Good to be Larry.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: No it's not.

jokes.text: I would wish you the best of luck, but I believe luck is a concept invented by the weak to explain their failures.
jokes.setup: Being asked to wish Tom luck
jokes.punchline: Ron's philosophical rejection of luck concept
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron Swanson philosophy delivered with complete sincerity. The structure and character integration are excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Luck is a concept invented by the weak to explain their failures. I don't believe in luck. I believe in preparation, and I believe in Ron Swanson.

jokes.text: Basically, if you're older than me, you're a grandpa. And if you are a grandpa, you're dead.
jokes.setup: Tom defending his 'grandpa' comments
jokes.punchline: Increasingly extreme age-based categories
jokes.explanation: Good Tom logic with escalating absurdity. The dead grandpa conclusion is perfectly extreme for him.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Look, I was just making observations. First of all, you're not a millennial anymore, you're a geriatric millennial.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Then you become a senior millennial, which is basically a boomer.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Then you're a proto-grandpa.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Then you're a full grandpa.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Then you're a mega grandpa.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Then you're a death grandpa, and that's when you die.

jokes.text: This check is made out to 'Lenny,' which I think is a typo for 'Larry,' which isn't even actually my name.
jokes.setup: Jerry receiving his profit share
jokes.punchline: Multiple layers of name confusion
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of the Jerry name running gag. The layered confusion is perfectly constructed.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I got my profit share check!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That's great, Jerry. How much?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It says here it's made out to 'Garry Gergich.'

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: That's not even close to your name.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I know! But I've been cashing checks with that name for three years now.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Wait, your actual name is...?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Larry. It's Larry. But everyone calls me Jerry.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: So your name is Larry, but we call you Jerry, and the government calls you Garry?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yes.

jokes.text: Do you know anything from The Requiem for a Dream soundtrack?
jokes.setup: Ordering a singing telegram for sad occasion
jokes.punchline: Requesting music from extremely depressing movie
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark reference that's completely inappropriate for a singing telegram. Shows Andy's pop culture knowledge in absurd context.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'd like to order a singing telegram for a funeral.

dialogue.speaker: Telegram Service
dialogue.line: Oh, I'm so sorry for your loss. What song would you like?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Do you have anything from Schindler's List?

jokes.text: But not the kind that snaps in half, maybe.
jokes.setup: Ben saying there'll be other salt packets
jokes.punchline: Leslie's specific concern about salt packet type
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie obsessiveness over tiny details. The specificity of 'snaps in half' makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: There'll be other salt packets.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: But will they be *iodized*?

jokes.text: I also engaged in inappropriate texting, sexting, and tex-mexting, which is where you send photos of your junk from the restroom of a chili's to go.
jokes.setup: Dexhart confessing to sex scandals
jokes.punchline: Made-up term with overly specific restaurant location
jokes.explanation: Brilliant made-up word that escalates the confession perfectly. The Chili's specificity makes it absurdly detailed.

dialogue.speaker: Dexhart
dialogue.line: I've had relations with a woman at Applebee's in the bathroom.

dialogue.speaker: Dexhart
dialogue.line: And I may have Applebee's'd her.

jokes.text: No. Really. Tell me. It gets me off when women tell me that.
jokes.setup: Dexhart asking if he's a bad boy
jokes.punchline: Turning rhetorical question into sexual request
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dexhart moment showing his complete lack of boundaries. The casual delivery makes it more disturbing/funny.

dialogue.speaker: Dexhart
dialogue.line: Am I a bad boy?

dialogue.speaker: Dexhart
dialogue.line: Because I've been very bad and I need to be punished.

jokes.text: Enrique Shockwave, Willie Dynamite, Lee Harvey Teabag... Lieutenant Wilhelm Von Penis.
jokes.setup: Listing pseudonyms used for sexting
jokes.punchline: Increasingly ridiculous fake names culminating in German military penis reference
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation with each name getting more absurd. 'Lee Harvey Teabag' and 'Lieutenant Wilhelm Von Penis' are brilliantly stupid.

dialogue.speaker: Dexhart
dialogue.line: I use aliases. Like... Dr. Schlongenberg.

dialogue.speaker: Dexhart
dialogue.line: Or Captain Dong Thunderstick.

dialogue.speaker: Dexhart
dialogue.line: Or... The Penetrator General Von Dongenstein.

jokes.text: It's basically a dumping ground for old shipping containers. It's like a raccoon shantytown.
jokes.setup: Describing Dexhart's district
jokes.punchline: Vivid description ending with 'raccoon shantytown'
jokes.explanation: Good visual description with 'raccoon shantytown' being a particularly vivid and funny image.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: It's basically a raccoon shantytown.

jokes.text: Come on, time is money, money is power, power is pizza, pizza is knowledge. Let's go.
jokes.setup: Tom rushing the business pitch meeting
jokes.punchline: Increasingly nonsensical chain of business logic
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom pseudo-business speak that starts logical but becomes completely absurd. 'Pizza is knowledge' is the perfect punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Okay, so we partner with a local restaurant, right? They give us 20% off appetizers. We take those savings and reinvest them into a premium smoothie line. The smoothie line attracts health-conscious millennials. Those millennials are also interested in cryptocurrency, so we pivot to blockchain consulting. Blockchain consulting leads us naturally into luxury watch repair. And luxury watch repair is just one step away from owning our own clock tower. Clock towers are basically just big Ben. Big Ben is in London. London is where the Royal Family lives. The Royal Family has a lot of money. Money is what we need. Therefore, this plan is foolproof.

jokes.text: So my idea is for an abacus, but on your phone. I call it a 'phone-bacus.'
jokes.setup: Entrepreneur pitching his idea
jokes.punchline: Describing a calculator as revolutionary invention with bad portmanteau
jokes.explanation: Good setup for bad entrepreneur stereotype. The portmanteau 'phone-bacus' is appropriately clunky.

dialogue.speaker: Entrepreneur
dialogue.line: This is going to revolutionize the way people do math. It's a calculator... combined with a vibrator. I call it the 'Calculator.'

jokes.text: Son, you just described a calculator.
jokes.setup: Greg's 'revolutionary' phone-bacus idea
jokes.punchline: Ron's blunt reality check
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron response with maximum economy. The 'Son' address makes it more condescending and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: A phone-bacus. So you've taken the two least efficient technologies of all time and combined them into one product.

jokes.text: Three words: Automated word counter. Get out. Two. Words. No. One. Word.
jokes.setup: Another bad business pitch
jokes.punchline: Tom's increasingly brief rejections culminating in single word
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation structure with perfect economy. The countdown from three to one word is masterfully executed.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Okay, so I've been thinking about starting a new business venture. It's called 'Rent-A-Swag.' You rent me, and I come to your event and I'm just... swag.

dialogue.speaker: Entrepreneur
dialogue.line: That's the worst idea I've ever heard.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, hear me out. What if we did it backwards? You pay me not to come?

dialogue.speaker: Entrepreneur
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: What if I just stood outside your business and looked cool?

dialogue.speaker: Entrepreneur
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: What if—

dialogue.speaker: Entrepreneur
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: I'm not gonna buy that thing. It's covered in a gallon of your boogers.
jokes.setup: Jerry sneezing all over his invention
jokes.punchline: Tom's disgusted rejection with exaggerated booger estimate
jokes.explanation: Gross-out humor that works with the visual setup. 'Gallon of boogers' is appropriately disgusting.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa! Did you just sneeze all over this? That's disgusting, man. There's like a million boogers on here.

jokes.text: Olive, rosemary, chicken. Fifty burritos.
jokes.setup: Listing cute girls' names
jokes.punchline: Names devolving into food items and quantities
jokes.explanation: Good pregnancy brain humor showing Ann's thoughts shifting to food. The escalation from herbs to burritos works.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Here are some cute girls' names: Emma, Olivia, Sophia...

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: And then it just becomes: Broccoli, Cantaloupe, Half a Chicken...

jokes.text: I'm pregnant, and I'm a little bit crazy. So if you don't bring our appetizers out in the next 30 seconds, I'm gonna plunge your face into the deep fryer!
jokes.setup: Ann explaining she's pregnant and crazy
jokes.punchline: Extremely violent threat followed by polite follow-up
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between violent threat and polite aftermath. Shows pregnant hormone swings effectively.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I'm pregnant and crazy. I will hurt you.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: But thank you for the offer.

jokes.text: What? He's kinda cute. Oh, God, Shauna, no. I-I just feel like I could fix him, you know?
jokes.setup: Shauna commenting on Dexhart's attractiveness
jokes.punchline: Classic 'I can fix him' delusion about terrible man
jokes.explanation: Relatable bad relationship choice humor. The 'I can fix him' line is universally recognizable.

dialogue.speaker: Shauna
dialogue.line: I know he's a terrible person, but I genuinely believe I could fix him. Like, I'm pretty sure all he needs is a good woman and he'd completely turn his life around.

dialogue.speaker: Shauna
dialogue.line: I'm not saying it would be easy, but I think with enough patience and love, I could change him into a decent human being.

jokes.text: Macaroni and poison. It's like mac and cheese, but with a special ingredient.
jokes.setup: Tom asking for business ideas
jokes.punchline: April's casual poisoning proposal with understated explanation
jokes.explanation: Perfect April darkness delivered with complete deadpan. The casual 'special ingredient' explanation makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: April, I need business ideas. What do you got?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Poison.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Poison? That's not a business.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: It could be. You could poison people, and then sell them the antidote.

jokes.text: Every socket set I've ever bought only goes up to 1/2 inch. I'd love a couple larger sizes. Take it all the way up to 5/8.
jokes.setup: Ron offering a business idea
jokes.punchline: Extremely specific woodworking tool complaint
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron character moment with genuine, practical concern delivered seriously. The specificity makes it funny.

jokes.text: Bears bouncing on trampoline roads.
jokes.setup: Andy offering a business idea
jokes.punchline: Completely nonsensical concept
jokes.explanation: Pure Andy randomness that's perfectly in character. The image is absurd enough to be memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, I've got a business idea. What if we made a restaurant, but get this... the food was invisible.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: And people would pay full price, but they'd just be eating air. We'd call it 'The Emperor's Table.'

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I think we'd make a killing.

jokes.text: Second chunce four Lesle! Perv Dexxxhrt equals... birthday mouse
jokes.setup: Leslie making a campaign banner from scraps
jokes.punchline: Multiple spelling errors and nonsensical message fragments
jokes.explanation: Excellent visual comedy showing Leslie's desperation. 'Birthday mouse' and misspellings make it absurdly pathetic.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I made a campaign banner from recycled materials I found behind the community center. It says 'Lesli Knope for City Council: We Can Do This Together' — wait, why does it say 'Lesli'? And what does 'Garbageman Forever' mean? That's not even... oh God, I think I glued newspaper clippings upside down.

jokes.text: Tiny little acorn penis? Forget it. A weird image. But one that does not diminish my enthusiasm.
jokes.setup: Chris talking about having a boy
jokes.punchline: Disturbing mental image followed by Chris's inability to be deterred
jokes.explanation: 'Acorn penis' is a wonderfully disturbing image, and Chris's unshakeable positivity makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I'm going to have a boy.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That's great, Chris!

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I know. I've been picturing it — me and my son, playing catch in the backyard, teaching him about nutrition and self-improvement.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: That's disturbing.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Why? What's disturbing about that?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I don't know, the image of you in shorts throwing a ball around.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Well, that's not going to stop me. I'm still going to do it.

jokes.text: Are we having a smurf?
jokes.setup: Trying to read illegible ultrasound results
jokes.punchline: Ann's confused interpretation of scribbled text
jokes.explanation: Good confusion comedy with 'smurf' being an appropriately absurd misreading.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I can't read this. What does this say? Is that a... is that a foot? Or a... or a butt?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It's definitely a foot.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Well, I don't know! I've never seen an ultrasound before. For all I know, the baby could be a blob.

jokes.text: Oh, I love the sound of silence before a big cheer. That's what's happening, right?
jokes.setup: Leslie announcing her campaign to complete silence
jokes.punchline: Hopeful misinterpretation of obvious rejection
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment showing Leslie's optimism in face of obvious opposition. The hopeful question marks it.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm running for City Council!

dialogue.speaker: Everyone
dialogue.line: [complete silence]

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: So that's a yes from everyone. Great! I can feel the energy in this room.

jokes.text: I would love it if you ran an insane campaign and basically turned into the joker. But that means you probably shouldn't do it.
jokes.setup: April giving Leslie advice about running
jokes.punchline: Wanting chaos but recognizing it's bad advice
jokes.explanation: Perfect April moment showing her love of chaos while being surprisingly wise. The Joker comparison is spot-on.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Look, I want to tell you to just wing it and embrace the chaos, but I know that's terrible advice. So instead I'm going to tell you the responsible thing: make a detailed plan, stick to it, and don't deviate. Also, I'm lying. Wing it. Chaos is fun.

jokes.text: I don't know, Leslie. It seems risky. And I'd hate to see you go through another tough fight. But I could be wrong. I haven't pooped in three days.
jokes.setup: Andy giving thoughtful advice about Leslie's campaign
jokes.punchline: Completely unrelated personal confession
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy moment mixing genuine concern with completely random overshare. The contrast makes it hilarious.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Leslie, you're going to do great in this campaign. You just need to believe in yourself and remember why you're doing this in the first place.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Also, I've been stealing office supplies for three years and I think someone finally noticed because Jerry asked me about my stapler yesterday.

jokes.text: What the hell do you know, Dum-Dum?
jokes.setup: Ron giving sage advice against running
jokes.punchline: Leslie's angry dismissal with childish insult
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Leslie's breakdown. Calling Ron 'Dum-Dum' is hilariously inappropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Leslie, you can't just run for office. It requires strategy, patience, and a willingness to compromise your principles.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, you're a big stupid butt, Ron!

jokes.text: I'm younger than you, and I already have my own department. Plus I'm married, and I make more money than you.
jokes.setup: April understanding why Tom is bummed
jokes.punchline: Listing all the ways she's more successful
jokes.explanation: Good April cruelty disguised as sympathy. The escalating list of advantages is appropriately mean.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You're upset because I'm more successful than you. I have a better job, I make more money, I have better health insurance, my car is newer, my apartment is nicer, I've read more books, I've been to more countries, I speak more languages, my skin is clearer, I'm taller, I'm faster, I'm stronger, and I'm significantly better looking.

jokes.text: Whatever. You just merged your department with parks then made up your own title.
jokes.setup: April bragging about her department
jokes.punchline: Tom's deflating reality check about her title
jokes.explanation: Good comeback that deflates April's bragging with accurate observation about her questionable promotion.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'm the Deputy Director of the Parks Department.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: You're the only one in the Parks Department.

jokes.text: I'm empty, yo. Fill me up. / Do that again.
jokes.setup: Tom's talking tissue product giving encouragement
jokes.punchline: Tom's unexpected enthusiasm for the bizarre product
jokes.explanation: Mildly funny absurd product concept. Tom's sudden interest is character-appropriate but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Talking Tissue
dialogue.line: You can do it!

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: It really works!

jokes.text: What do you say you and I get together in a special way? I-I'm pregnant. The more the merrier.
jokes.setup: Jean-Ralphio hitting on pregnant Ann
jokes.punchline: Doubling down when told she's pregnant
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jean-Ralphio inappropriateness. His inability to be deterred by pregnancy is hilariously wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Hey baby, what's your name?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I'm pregnant.

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: That's cool, that's cool. So what do you say we get out of here?

jokes.text: I guess I'm open-minded as hell.
jokes.setup: Jean-Ralphio calling both Ann and Chris 'beautiful'
jokes.punchline: His proud self-assessment of his 'open-mindedness'
jokes.explanation: Good Jean-Ralphio moment where his sleaziness is reframed as progressive open-mindedness.

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: I'm very open-minded. I call 'em like I see 'em. Ann, you're beautiful. Chris, you're beautiful.

jokes.text: Also, if you don't know already, there's a malfunction with the TV, where it keeps ordering porno, like, a ton of porno. Like, how can someone watch that much porno in one sitting? Only when you're out of the house.
jokes.setup: Kid talking to his dad about TV 'malfunction'
jokes.punchline: Increasingly detailed explanation that reveals he's ordering porn
jokes.explanation: Good escalating awkwardness as the kid reveals more about his porn ordering while calling it a malfunction.

dialogue.speaker: Saperstein's son
dialogue.line: Dad, the TV is broken. It keeps showing these weird channels.

dialogue.speaker: Saperstein's son
dialogue.line: Well, I was looking for ESPN, but I accidentally hit some buttons and now there's all these... instructional videos.

dialogue.speaker: Saperstein's son
dialogue.line: They're about, um, physical education. Very physical. And there's a lot of heavy breathing.

dialogue.speaker: Saperstein's son
dialogue.line: The people in them aren't wearing much, and they keep calling me 'son' which is weird because you're the only one supposed to call me that.

dialogue.speaker: Saperstein's son
dialogue.line: Oh, and I may have used your credit card to unlock the premium channels. I thought it would fix the malfunction.

jokes.text: Do you really wanna spend any of that time talking about my mother and her 19-year-old Korean husband?
jokes.setup: Ben asking Jen how she's doing
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of mother's inappropriate relationship
jokes.explanation: Good Jen moment showing her cynical efficiency and casually dropping family dysfunction.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: How are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Jen
dialogue.line: Oh, you know, same old same old. My mom's been seeing this guy who's... well, let's just say he's closer to my age than hers.

jokes.text: That's how I enter rooms now. It's more dramatic.
jokes.setup: Tom bursting through doors
jokes.punchline: Explaining his new dramatic entrance policy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom self-importance and attention-seeking behavior explained matter-of-factly.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I've implemented a new dramatic entrance policy. From now on, I burst through doors like I'm about to deliver life-changing news.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: It makes me seem more important and keeps people on their toes.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Plus, it's great for my personal brand.

jokes.text: Clear them sinuses, playboy! Someone's got the sniffies. Tell your mama you love 'er. Step up your vitamin C game, bro!
jokes.setup: Tom's talking tissue invention
jokes.punchline: Series of inappropriately familiar and random motivational messages
jokes.explanation: Good absurd invention with appropriately random and overly familiar tissue messages. Very Tom.

dialogue.speaker: Talking Tissue
dialogue.line: You've got this, buddy! Now blow your nose, champ!

dialogue.speaker: Talking Tissue
dialogue.line: Feeling better? Of course you are, because you're a winner!

dialogue.speaker: Talking Tissue
dialogue.line: Remember that time you almost asked out Jennifer? Me too. You blew it, pal.

dialogue.speaker: Talking Tissue
dialogue.line: But hey, there's always tomorrow! Unless you get sick again. Then it's just tissues and regret.

jokes.text: Those terrible people have paid me so much money, I have a condo in every virgin island.
jokes.setup: Jen explaining how terrible people defeat good people
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of Virgin Islands real estate empire
jokes.explanation: Good dark comedy showing how Jen profits from political corruption. The Virgin Islands detail is perfectly excessive.

dialogue.speaker: Jen Barkley
dialogue.line: The reason that terrible people seem to win is because good people assume the worst won't happen. They don't prepare for it. Terrible people, on the other hand, they're always ready. They're always planning. I once knew a man who tried to take over the Virgin Islands with a real estate empire.

jokes.text: Be the woman who moves away, climbs the ladder, and then confidently comes back and has sex with her hot old English teacher just for kicks.
jokes.setup: Jen giving life advice about success
jokes.punchline: Advice escalating to inappropriate teacher relationship
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from motivational advice to inappropriate confession. Very Jen to make it about herself.

dialogue.speaker: Jen
dialogue.line: You know what the secret to success is? You gotta find a mentor. Someone older, wiser, who can guide you through life.

dialogue.speaker: Jen
dialogue.line: Build a relationship with them. Spend time together. Get to know them on a personal level.

dialogue.speaker: Jen
dialogue.line: And if they're your high school teacher, well, even better. The dynamics are already there.

jokes.text: I don't care enough about you to lie.
jokes.setup: Jen telling Leslie to trust her advice
jokes.punchline: Brutal honesty about not caring about Leslie
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jen line that's both reassuring and insulting. Maximum economy and character truth. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Jen
dialogue.line: You should really trust my advice on this.

dialogue.speaker: Jen
dialogue.line: I'm going to be brutally honest with you: I don't care about you at all.

jokes.text: If you say one more word, you will legally owe me $1,200, and I will sue you. Let's not end it like that.
jokes.setup: Jen's expensive hourly rate and Leslie wanting to keep talking
jokes.punchline: Threatening lawsuit for single word while trying to be friendly
jokes.explanation: Good Jen moment showing her cutthroat business approach disguised as friendship. The contrast works well.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Jen, I know your hourly rate is very expensive, so I'll keep this brief.

dialogue.speaker: Jen Barkley
dialogue.line: Thank you.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: But I just wanted to say how much I appreciate everything you've done, and I think you're really great, and—

dialogue.speaker: Jen Barkley
dialogue.line: Leslie, that's going to cost you $500.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: What? For what?

dialogue.speaker: Jen Barkley
dialogue.line: For me listening to you talk. You owe me money for every word you say.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Oh my God, okay, okay. I'm done. Bye, Jen. You're the best!

dialogue.speaker: Jen Barkley
dialogue.line: That's $50,000. I'm suing you.

jokes.text: I thought you didn't have a preference. I didn't. I just really wanted it to be a boy. Me too. I don't know why.
jokes.setup: Both claiming no gender preference
jokes.punchline: Immediately admitting they both wanted a boy for no reason
jokes.explanation: Relatable parent humor about secretly having preferences despite claiming otherwise. The honesty is endearing.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: We don't care if it's a boy or a girl. We just want a healthy baby.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Exactly. Gender is completely irrelevant to us.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Although, a boy would be nice.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Yeah, a boy would be great.

jokes.text: Would you be interested in adopting my children? They're in their late 20s, they're terrible.
jokes.setup: Dr. Saperstein praising Chris and Ann
jokes.punchline: Offering his adult children for adoption with brutal assessment
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurd concept with perfect delivery. Adult adoption offer with honest assessment of his own children is hilarious.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Saperstein
dialogue.line: Chris and Ann, you two are going to make wonderful parents. In fact, I'd like to offer my own children for adoption. They're adults now, so there shouldn't be any legal issues. Jerry's a disappointment, Millicent's a bigger disappointment, and Craig... well, Craig's in prison.

jokes.text: A shockingly unshocking press conference in which a recently unemployed woman said nothing of importance.
jokes.setup: Perd reporting on Leslie's press conference
jokes.punchline: Perd's characteristically redundant and obvious summary
jokes.explanation: Perfect Perd reporting style with redundant 'shockingly unshocking' and obvious content summary.

dialogue.speaker: Perd Hapley
dialogue.line: Well, I'm Perd Hapley, and I just witnessed a press conference. What happened at the press conference? A woman spoke. What did the woman do? She pressed. What did she press? Matters. Were there members of the press there? Yes, there were, and that's why it was called a press conference.

tmdb_overview: Leslie and Ben both face new challenges on the first day of new jobs. Donna, April and Andy play a prank on Ben, while Tom prepares for a big presentation. Meanwhile Ann and Chris contemplate taking a big leap together.
insight: 66 jokes in 27 minutes but escalation-driven absurdism keeps impact modest at 6.61.
